courses.title: Mastering Data Structures & Algorithms
courses.description: A comprehensive guide to understanding lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, and dynamic programming.

lessons.title: Introduction to Complexity & Big O
lessons.content: Big O notation measures the worst-case time or space requirement of an algorithm as a function of the input size. Constant O(1), Linear O(n), Logarithmic O(log n), Quadratic O(n^2). Understanding these curves is fundamental to writing performant code.

lessons.title: Singly & Doubly Linked Lists
lessons.content: A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are not stored in contiguous memory locations. Singly linked list nodes contain data and a pointer to the next node, while doubly linked list nodes also contain a pointer to the previous node.

courses.title: Java Backend Development with Spring Boot
courses.description: Build robust, scalable enterprise backends using modern Java, MVC routing, RESTful APIs, Hibernate, and MongoDB.

lessons.title: Introduction to Spring Boot & MVC
lessons.content: Spring Boot simplifies the creation of stand-alone, production-grade Spring applications. Learn about Dependency Injection, @RestController, and HTTP request routing methods.

lessons.title: Designing REST APIs & Dependency Injection
lessons.content: REST API design principles utilize standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and status codes (200, 201, 400, 404, 500) to represent system state securely. Dependency Injection decouples components dynamically at run-time.

courses.title: Deep Learning & Generative AI Systems
courses.description: Learn the architecture of Transformer networks, fine-tuning large language models, prompt engineering, and RAG architectures.

lessons.title: Attention is All You Need: Transformer Mechanics
lessons.content: The Transformer architecture revolutionized machine learning by removing recurrent networks (RNNs) entirely, introducing Self-Attention layers that enable parallel calculation of contextual embeddings for sequence elements.

lessons.title: Prompt Engineering & RAG Architecture
lessons.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) integrates dense document indices or vector DB lookups directly into standard prompting schemas to ground models contextually, avoiding hallucinations and training costs.

courses.title: System Design & Scalable Architectures
courses.description: Learn high-level system design patterns, distributed consensus algorithms, horizontal scaling strategies, Memcached/Redis, and proxy layers.

lessons.title: Monolith vs Microservices & API Gateways
lessons.content: Understanding the trade-offs between monolithic architectures and service-oriented / microservices ecosystems. Learn about routing protocols, service discovery, and the API Gateway pattern.

lessons.title: Caching Strategies with Redis & Memcached
lessons.content: Learn how in-memory key-value databases optimize lookup speeds and prevent database starvation. Understand cache-aside, write-through, write-behind patterns, and eviction schemes like LRU.

courses.title: Database Systems & MongoDB NoSQL Administration
courses.description: Master document-oriented storage paradigms, flexible schema designs, indexing strategies, sharding, and high availability clusters using MongoDB.

lessons.title: Relational SQL vs NoSQL MongoDB Paradigms
lessons.content: A comparative deep dive between relational databases and JSON document databases. Contrast strict schemas, normalization, and ACID properties against rich, nestable key-value document records.

lessons.title: Indexing, Sharding, & High Availability in MongoDB
lessons.content: Configure composite indices, geo-spatial indexes, horizontal partitioning of datasets using shard keys, and robust self-healing replica sets to assure maximum operational uptime.

quizzes.title: Module 1 Assessment: Complexity Analysis
quizzes.description: Test your understanding of Big O time complexity and primary data structures.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of inserting an element into a Singly Linked List at the end, if we only have a pointer to the head?
questions.explanation: Since we only have a pointer to the head node, we must traverse all n elements of the list to reach the end. Therefore, it takes O(n) time.

questions.question: Which data structure operates on a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) model?
questions.explanation: A Stack utilizes a LIFO structure where the last item inserted (pushed) is the first item removed (popped).

quizzes.title: AI Quiz: OOP basics
quizzes.description: A customized assessment on OOP basics (Difficulty: Medium)

questions.question: Regarding OOP basics, what is the fundamental time complexity of a standard search operation?
questions.explanation: In the worst-case, searching requires scanning through the structural collection elements, yielding an O(n) complexity bounds.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a primary advantage of utilizing OOP basics structures over arrays?
options: Random element access in O(1) time
questions.explanation: Dynamic allocations allow structures to expand and shrink dynamically, and relative pointers allow easy item insertions and deletions without shifting adjacent memory blocks.

questions.question: Which of the following operations is typically slowest when using standard OOP basics structures?
options: Checking if the structure is empty
questions.explanation: Accessing the k-th element requires linear scanning from the head node, which takes O(k) time, whereas head operations are usually O(1).

quizzes.title: Dynamic Quiz: Linked Lists
quizzes.description: A customized assessment on Linked Lists (Difficulty: undefined)

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of Floyd's Cycle-Finding Algorithm (Tortoise and Hare) over using a Hash Set to detect a cycle in a singly linked list?
options: It reduces the time complexity from O(N) to O(log N).
It reduces the auxiliary space complexity from O(N) to O(1).
It can detect cycles in doubly linked lists, whereas Hash Sets only work for singly linked lists.
It guarantees that the exact node where the cycle begins is identified without any additional steps.
questions.explanation: A hash set requires storing references/pointers to all visited nodes, which takes O(N) auxiliary space. Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm uses only two pointers (slow and fast moving at different speeds), thereby reducing the auxiliary space complexity to O(1) while maintaining the O(N) time complexity.

questions.question: You are given a pointer to a node 'N' in the middle of a singly linked list. You do NOT have access to the head of the list. Which of the following is the most viable method to 'delete' this node, and what is its main limitation?
options: Copy the data from the next node (N.next) into N, then set N.next to N.next.next. This does not work if N is the last node in the list.
Traverse backward from N to find its predecessor, then update its predecessor's next pointer. This works for all nodes but takes O(N) time.
Set N = N.next. This works for all nodes and runs in O(1) time.
Swap the head of the list with N, then delete the head. This works for all nodes in O(1) time.
questions.explanation: Because we don't have access to the head, we cannot easily find N's predecessor to bypass N. The standard workaround is copying N.next's value into N, and deleting N.next by pointing N's next pointer to N.next.next. This approach fails if N is the last node (tail) because there is no succeeding node from which to copy data.

questions.question: Consider the standard iterative algorithm to reverse a singly linked list using three pointers: 'prev', 'curr', and 'next_node'. Inside the traversal loop (while curr is not null), which sequence of pointer updates correctly reverses the current node's link and advances the pointers?
options: curr.next = prev; prev = curr; curr = next_node; next_node = curr.next;
next_node = curr.next; curr.next = prev; prev = curr; curr = next_node;
next_node = curr.next; prev = curr; curr.next = prev; curr = next_node;
curr.next = prev; next_node = curr.next; prev = curr; curr = next_node;
questions.explanation: To safely reverse the current link without losing reference to the rest of the list, you must first save the next node: 'next_node = curr.next'. Then, reverse the link: 'curr.next = prev'. Finally, shift the pointers forward for the next iteration: 'prev = curr' and then 'curr = next_node'.

quizzes.title: AI generated Quiz: Docker Containers
quizzes.description: A customized assessment on Docker Containers (Difficulty: Medium)

questions.question: What is the difference between a Docker Image and a Docker Container?
options: An image is a read-only blueprint; a container is a runnable instance of that blueprint
An image runs on Windows; a container runs on Linux
Containers are compiled; images are interpreted
Containers are saved to DockerHub; images reside only on local host filesystems
questions.explanation: A Docker Image is a static, read-only template with instructions for creating a container. A Container is the active, isolated process running that image.

questions.question: Which Dockerfile instruction specifies the default command executed when a container starts?
questions.explanation: CMD defines the default command and arguments to execute when the container is run. RUN is used during the image build step to install dependencies.

questions.question: What Docker concept allows persistent file storage independent of a container's lifecycle?
questions.explanation: Docker Volumes bypass the copy-on-write filesystem of containers and write directly to the host filesystem, preserving data when containers are deleted.

quizzes.title: AI generated Quiz: String
quizzes.description: A customized assessment on String (Difficulty: Medium)

questions.question: Which data structure operates on a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) access strategy?
questions.explanation: A stack is a linear structure where insertions and deletions happen at the same end (the top), adhering to Last-In, First-Out (LIFO).

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using a Hash Map?
options: Provides O(1) average-time complexity for search, insertion, and deletion
Guarantees thread safety in concurrent environments
questions.explanation: Hash maps use hashing to map keys directly to buckets, enabling near-instant search, insertion, and lookup in O(1) average time.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm achieves O(n log n) worst-case time complexity and is stable?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort recursively divides the collection, sorting halves, and merging. It guarantees O(n log n) runtime in all cases and preserves ordering of equal elements (stable).

quizzes.title: AI generated Quiz: Dynamic Programming
quizzes.description: A customized assessment on Dynamic Programming (Difficulty: Medium)

questions.question: What are the two key attributes that a problem must exhibit to be solved using Dynamic Programming?
options: Divide and conquer structure & recursive definition
questions.explanation: Dynamic programming requires overlapping subproblems (recomputing the same sub-problems repeatedly) and optimal substructure (optimal solution to the global problem can be constructed from optimal solutions to subproblems).

questions.question: What is the difference between Memoization and Tabulation?
options: Memoization is top-down (recursive); Tabulation is bottom-up (iterative)
Memoization is bottom-up (iterative); Tabulation is top-down (recursive)
Memoization is O(N^2); Tabulation is O(N)
Memoization uses no additional space; Tabulation uses an auxiliary array
questions.explanation: Memoization caches recursive results (top-down), while Tabulation fills a table/array sequentially from base cases upwards (bottom-up).

questions.question: In the 0/1 Knapsack problem, why does the greedy approach fail?
options: Items cannot be divided; we must take a full item or leave it
The sorting of weight ratio takes O(N^2) complexity
The item values must be sorted descending
questions.explanation: Since items cannot be split, a greedy choice based on value-to-weight ratio may result in suboptimal space usage. DP searches the full combination space to find the absolute optimum.

questions.question: Which annotation combination is shorthand for @Controller and @ResponseBody?
questions.explanation: @RestController is a convenience annotation that marks a class as a controller where every method returns a domain object directly as HTTP response body serialized to JSON/XML.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Spring MVC's DispatcherServlet?
options: Act as the Front Controller, routing incoming requests to handlers
Compile Java classes into runtime bytecode
questions.explanation: DispatcherServlet acts as the central coordinator for HTTP request processing, receiving requests and dispatching them to the correct Spring MVC Controllers.

questions.question: How do you bind an HTTP request query parameter to a controller method argument?
questions.explanation: @RequestParam binds query parameters (e.g. ?name=Alex) to Java method variables. @PathVariable binds segment values directly within the URL route.

codingProblems.title: Two Sum
codingProblems.description:
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.

codingProblems.title: Reverse a Linked List

codingProblems.title: Valid Parentheses
codingProblems.description:
Given a string `s` containing just the characters `'('`, `')'`, `'{'`, `'}'`, `'['` and `']'`, determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:
1. Open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets.
2. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
3. Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.

codingProblems.title: Merge Intervals
codingProblems.description: Given an array of `intervals` where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]`, merge all overlapping intervals, and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.

codingProblems.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
codingProblems.description: Given a string `s`, return the longest palindromic substring in `s`.

codingProblems.title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane's Algorithm)
codingProblems.description: Given an integer array `nums`, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one number) which has the largest sum and return its sum.

codingProblems.title: Container With Most Water
codingProblems.description: Given `n` non-negative integers `height` where each represents a point at coordinate `(i, height[i])`. Find two lines that together with the x-axis forms a container, such that the container contains the most water. Return the maximum area of water.

codingProblems.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
codingProblems.description: Given an integer array `nums` sorted in ascending order (with distinct values) which is possibly rotated, and a target value `target`. If target is found in the array return its index, otherwise, return `-1`.

codingProblems.title: Climbing Stairs
codingProblems.description: You are climbing a staircase. It takes `n` steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?

codingProblems.title: Product of Array Except Self
codingProblems.description: Given an integer array `nums`, return an array `answer` such that `answer[i]` is equal to the product of all the elements of `nums` except `nums[i]` in O(N) time and without using division.

codingProblems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
codingProblems.description:
You are given an array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price of a given stock on the `i`-th day. You want to maximize your profit by choosing a single day to buy one stock and choosing a different day in the future to sell that stock.

Return the maximum profit you can achieve. If you cannot achieve any profit, return `0`.

codingProblems.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
codingProblems.description: Given a binary search tree (BST), find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) node of two given nodes `p` and `q` in the BST.

codingProblems.title: Trapping Rain Water
codingProblems.description: Given `n` non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.

codingProblems.title: Course Schedule (Topological Sort)
codingProblems.description:
There are a total of `numCourses` courses you have to take, labeled from `0` to `numCourses - 1`. You are given an array `prerequisites` where `prerequisites[i] = [a, b]` indicates that you must take course `b` first if you want to take course `a`.

Return `true` if you can finish all courses. Otherwise, return `false`.

codingProblems.title: Edit Distance
codingProblems.description:
Given two strings `word1` and `word2`, return the minimum number of operations required to convert `word1` to `word2`.

You have the following three operations permitted on a word:
1. Insert a character
2. Delete a character
3. Replace a character

codingProblems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
codingProblems.description: Given a string `s`, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

codingProblems.title: Number of Islands
codingProblems.description: Given an `m x n` 2D binary grid `grid` which represents a map of `'1'`s (land) and `'0'`s (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.

codingProblems.title: Decode String
codingProblems.description:
Given an encoded string, return its decoded string.

The encoding rule is: `k[encoded_string]`, where the `encoded_string` inside the square brackets is being repeated exactly `k` times. You may assume that the input string is always valid; no extra white spaces, square brackets are well-formed, etc.

codingProblems.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
codingProblems.description: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, return the `k`-th largest element in the array. Note that it is the `k`-th largest element in the sorted order, not the `k`-th distinct element.

codingProblems.title: Subsets (Power Set)
codingProblems.description: Given an integer array `nums` of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets. Return the solution in any order.

codingProblems.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
codingProblems.description: Given the `root` of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, level by level).

codingProblems.title: LCS: Longest Common Subsequence
codingProblems.description: Given two strings `text1` and `text2`, return the length of their longest common subsequence. If there is no common subsequence, return 0.

codingProblems.title: House Robber
codingProblems.description:
You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed, the only constraint stopping you from robbing each of them is that adjacent houses have security systems connected and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night.

Given an integer array `nums` representing the amount of money of each house, return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.

codingProblems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
codingProblems.description: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, return the `k` most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.

codingProblems.title: Coin Change
codingProblems.description: You are given an integer array `coins` representing coins of different denominations and an integer `amount` representing a total amount of money. Return the fewest number of coins that you need to make up that amount. If that amount of money cannot be made up by any combination of the coins, return `-1`.

codingProblems.title: Word Search (DFS Grid Search)
codingProblems.description: Given an `m x n` grid of characters `board` and a string `word`, return `true` if `word` exists in the grid. The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells, where adjacent cells are horizontally or vertically neighboring. The same letter cell may not be used more than once.

codingProblems.title: Minimum Window Substring
codingProblems.description: Given two strings `s` and `t` of lengths `m` and `n` respectively, return the minimum window substring of `s` such that every character in `t` (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string `""`.

codingProblems.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
codingProblems.description: Given two sorted arrays `nums1` and `nums2` of size `m` and `n` respectively, return the median of the two sorted arrays. The overall run time complexity should be O(log(m+n)).

codingProblems.title: Merge K Sorted Lists
codingProblems.description: You are given an array of `k` linked-lists `lists`, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list and return it.

codingProblems.title: Binary Tree Postorder Traversal
codingProblems.description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the postorder traversal of its nodes' values.
